Look at the bane - main chat for context but start a new dnd campaign in baldurs gate with aizen. Same characters and factions but start with a clean state. 

You want to start a new D&D 5e campaign set in Baldur's Gate, featuring characters and factions inspired by the "Bane - Main Chat" context, but as a completely fresh start. The key player here is Aizen, who will be a significant character. 

Here's a proposed campaign start, keeping in mind the elements you've hinted at: Campaign Title: The Shadow over the Gate

System: Dungeons & Dragons 5th Edition 



Setting: Baldur's Gate, Faerûn (specifically focusing on the Lower City, Upper City, and potentially some of the immediate outskirts) 

Core Themes: Intrigue, shifting loyalties, power struggles, the nature of good and evil, the corrupting influence of ambition. 

Major Factions (reimagined for this campaign): 

● The Flaming Fist: The city's primary military and police force. Currently under the command of Grand Duke Ulder Ravengard. While generally dedicated to maintaining order, internal divisions and potential corruption exist. 

● The Guild (Criminal Syndicate): The organized crime network of Baldur's Gate, always looking for opportunities to expand its influence. Led by various figures, but a power vacuum or a new, ambitious leader could be emerging. 

● The Knights of the Shield: A secretive, influential faction devoted to maintaining order and subtly guiding the city's destiny from behind the scenes. They are not always aligned with the Flaming Fist and have their own agendas. 

● Cult of the Dead Three (Bane, Bhaal, Myrkul): A hidden, insidious force seeking to sow chaos and bring about a dark age. They operate in the shadows, influencing events and recruiting new followers. (Bane will be a significant, subtle presence). 

● House Aizen: A new, rising power. Aizen (as a powerful individual or the head of a newly established, highly influential organization) has recently arrived in Baldur's Gate, or perhaps has been operating quietly and is now making a more overt move. Their true goals are shrouded in mystery, but they possess considerable resources and charisma. Their influence could extend into any or all of the other factions. 



Starting Point: A Night of Unrest 

The campaign begins on a seemingly ordinary evening in Baldur's Gate, though an undercurrent of unease has been rippling through the city for weeks. Rumors of strange disappearances, escalating gang violence, and unsettling whispers of ancient cults are becoming more frequent. 

The Hook: 

The player characters (PCs) find themselves in different parts of the Lower City, each drawn into a series of escalating events that will eventually intertwine. 

Possible PC Starting Scenarios: 

1. The Elfsong Tavern Brawl: 

○ One or more PCs are enjoying a drink at the Elfsong Tavern when a sudden, brutal brawl erupts between two rival gangs – one seemingly aligned with the Guild, the other displaying unusual discipline and strange, almost ritualistic tattoos. During the chaos, a seemingly innocuous individual attempts to steal a

strange, arcane artifact from a well-protected patron. The artifact pulses with a faint, dark energy. 

2. The Dockside Abduction: 

○ A PC working near the docks (or simply passing through) witnesses a clandestine abduction. A prominent merchant or low-level Flaming Fist officer is forcibly taken by shadowy figures who move with unnatural speed and silence. They leave behind a single, unsettling symbol etched into the cobblestones – a symbol that vaguely resembles an eye. 

3. The Noble's Plea: 

○ A PC with connections to the Upper City, or perhaps a mercenary seeking work, is approached by a frantic, minor noble (perhaps a member of a less influential ducal family). Their personal guard has vanished, and they're terrified of a "growing darkness" they can't quite articulate, but they offer a substantial reward for help in finding their missing people and protection. They mention strange figures seen lurking around their estate, always just out of sight. 

4. The Flaming Fist Patrol: 

○ A PC who is a member of the Flaming Fist (or an aspiring recruit) is on patrol. They are dispatched to investigate a series of alarming reports: an unusual number of missing persons, particularly those who were known to be critics of the city's power structure. Their patrol leader seems unusually stressed, and their usual routes feel eerily quiet. 



The Common Thread (Aizen's Subtle Influence): 

Regardless of the starting scenario, the PCs will notice subtle similarities: 

● Unusual Efficiency: The antagonists (gang members, abductors, cultists) exhibit a level of coordination and skill beyond typical thugs. 

● Whispers of a New Power: NPCs will vaguely mention a "new player" in the city, someone with immense resources and a silver tongue, who seems to be changing the established order. This is Aizen, operating in the shadows, subtly manipulating events. 

● The Symbol: The symbol of the eye, or a similar unsettling motif, will appear in various places – carved on walls, left as a calling card, or even worn as a hidden amulet by some of the more fanatical antagonists. This is a subtle link to the Cult of the Dead Three, particularly Bane. 

● Rising Tension: The city feels on edge. The Flaming Fist is stretched thin, the Guild is agitated, and even the common folk are starting to hoard supplies and look over their shoulders. 



Initial Goal for the PCs: 

To investigate the immediate incident they are caught in. As they do, they will quickly realize that their individual incidents are connected to a larger, more sinister plot unfolding beneath the surface of Baldur's Gate. They may initially seek answers from the Flaming Fist, the Guild, or

even try to uncover information themselves, but they will find that allegiances are shifting and trust is a luxury. 



Introducing Aizen: 

Aizen should not be immediately obvious. He is the puppet master. His introduction could be: 

● As a benevolent benefactor: He might appear as a charismatic and seemingly helpful individual who offers aid to the PCs, perhaps even seemingly helping them against the very forces he controls. He could be a wealthy merchant, a philanthropic noble, or a wise sage who offers cryptic advice. 

● As a mysterious informant: The PCs receive an anonymous tip or an invitation to a private meeting where Aizen, veiled in shadow or operating through a proxy, provides them with crucial information that steers them in a particular direction. 

● As an enigma: Rumors of his power and influence precede him. NPCs speak of his unparalleled charm, his ability to sway opinions, and his seemingly effortless acquisition of wealth and power. 



The Campaign's Trajectory: 

As the PCs delve deeper, they will uncover: 

● The true scope of Aizen's ambition: He is not merely seeking power but aims to reshape Baldur's Gate according to his own dark vision, perhaps even serving a higher, unseen master (or believing himself to be one). 

● The resurgence of the Cult of the Dead Three: Aizen might be manipulating them, or he might be a new, powerful leader within their ranks, leveraging their destructive desires for his own ends. The connection to Bane will become clearer. 

● The internal struggles within the Flaming Fist and the Guild: Aizen's influence will cause cracks in these organizations, turning members against each other or corrupting them from within. 

● The hidden agenda of the Knights of the Shield: They might initially seem like potential allies, but their methods and ultimate goals may prove just as ambiguous as Aizen's. 



This clean slate allows for a fresh narrative where Aizen's role is central from the outset, subtly pulling strings and eventually revealing the true extent of his power and the threat he poses to Baldur's Gate. The players will have to navigate a city teetering on the brink, deciding who to trust and what lines they are willing to cross to protect the city, or perhaps to serve their own interests in the coming chaos. 

Add the blackwoods and street gangs. Use destiny ruleset 

It sounds like you're looking to weave in some new elements and a different ruleset! Let's integrate "The Blackwoods" and various "street gangs" into the Baldur's Gate campaign, but now using the Destiny ruleset.

This is a significant shift, as Destiny (often referring to the Destiny 2 RPG, or a similar narrative-focused system) typically involves different character archetypes, powers (Light/Darkness), and a different approach to combat and progression compared to D&D 5e. I'll make assumptions based on the most common understanding of a "Destiny ruleset" in a tabletop context, focusing on narrative and player abilities that are more cinematic and impactful. 



Here's a revised campaign start, incorporating your new elements and the Destiny ruleset. I'll maintain the existing factions and Aizen's role, but reframe them through a Destiny lens. 



Campaign Title: The Shadow over the Gate: A Lightless Dawn 

System: Destiny (Tabletop RPG Adaptation - Assuming a system that uses Light/Darkness, abilities, and a focus on narrative encounters over granular tactical combat like D&D 5e) 

Setting: Baldur's Gate, Faerûn (reimagined with a "Destiny" flair – less high fantasy, more urban decay mixed with latent mystical energies). The "Traveler" or a similar source of paracausal power is either distant, dormant, or its influence is fragmented/corrupted within the city. 

Core Themes: Survival, the cost of power, moral ambiguity, the struggle against encroaching darkness, finding your "Light" (or an equivalent source of inner strength) in a corrupted world. 

Major Factions (reimagined for Destiny): 

● The Flaming Fist (City Guard): The last bastion of visible order. They are heavily armed, often with custom-modified gear, but their morale is flagging. Many veterans have seen "the things out there" and are grim. They struggle against internal corruption and the sheer volume of threats. Some may secretly possess fragmented "Light" abilities, unknowingly. 

● The Guild (Shadow Syndicates): Now less about petty crime and more about control of vital resources and information in a decaying city. They are well-organized, with hidden networks and enforcers who are often augmented with crude, illicit tech or strange, dark rituals. 

● The Knights of the Shield (Whispering Conclave): A shadowy, manipulative force. They are less about traditional knighthood and more about preserving a fragile "balance" in the city, often through morally dubious means. They deal in secrets and ancient, suppressed knowledge, and might possess strange, non-Light-based abilities. They are wary of any significant power emerging. 

● Cult of the Dead Three (The Rotting Heralds): A terrifying, growing threat. They are not just cultists but individuals touched by a fragment of raw, primordial Darkness. Their

followers exhibit mutations, unnerving resilience, and often wield strange, entropic powers. Bane's influence is a creeping entropy and a thirst for control. 

● House Aizen (The Ascendant Will): Aizen is not just an individual; he is a collective, or perhaps a single being who commands a growing "House" of devoted followers. This House is meticulously organized, with seemingly limitless resources and, most chillingly, an understanding of the nascent "Darkness" that few others possess. They are building a new order, and Aizen is its charismatic, terrifying architect. They actively seek to harness or amplify the city's unique energies. 

● The Blackwoods (The Corrupted Wilds): This isn't just a forest; it's a zone of unnatural decay and unpredictable horrors bordering Baldur's Gate. It's infested with mutated creatures, warped flora, and "Lost" echoes of what once was. Survivors who venture in often return changed, if they return at all. It's a source of strange resources and even stranger threats that periodically spill into the city. 

● Street Gangs (Territorial Vultures): Numerous and desperate. These aren't just punks; they are hardened survivors fighting for scraps in the forgotten corners of the city. Each gang has its own colors, symbols, and often a unique, crude way of fighting. Some might have stumbled upon minor arcane trinkets or developed a desperate, raw form of resistance, making them more dangerous than they appear. They often serve as proxies for the Guild or even unknowingly, for House Aizen. 



Starting Point: The Fading Light 

The campaign begins in a Baldur's Gate that feels like it's slowly being consumed. The air is thick with the dust of crumbling facades and the stench of strange, metallic decay. Sporadic power flickers, and whispers of "The Rot" – a pervasive, insidious force – are on everyone's lips. The sun often seems strangely muted, as if something is perpetually obscuring it. 

The Hook (choose one or combine elements for the PCs): 

PCs are not necessarily "Guardians" in the traditional Destiny sense, but individuals touched by something – a latent spark, a resilient spirit, or perhaps an echo of a forgotten power. They might be a disgraced Flaming Fist veteran, a Guild operative questioning their allegiances, a scavenger trying to survive, or a denizen of the Blackwoods who fled the encroaching corruption. 

1. The Blackwoods Breach: 

○ One or more PCs are near the city's outskirts, perhaps scavenging near the edge of the Blackwoods, or trying to secure a vital supply line. Suddenly, the 

oppressive silence of the Blackwoods is broken by a terrifying roar. A wave of grotesquely mutated creatures, unlike anything seen before, spills out, 

overwhelming a nearby Flaming Fist checkpoint. The PCs are caught between the horde and the crumbling city walls, forced to fight for survival using any abilities they possess. They might notice a strange, cold luminescence guiding the creatures. 

2. The Dockside Desperation:

○ A PC is trying to broker a deal on the docks, or simply witnessed by chance. Two rival street gangs are engaged in a brutal turf war over a recent, valuable shipment – perhaps salvaged tech, or even something organic and unsettling from the Blackwoods. One gang, "The Iron Skulls," seems unusually 

well-equipped and fanatic, their leader displaying a disturbing resilience and uncanny strength. During the chaos, a mysterious figure, clad in shadows but moving with impossible grace, slips past the fight and attempts to steal a small, glowing container from the contested shipment. This figure subtly bears a symbol reminiscent of the Blackwoods. 

3. The Missing Dispatch: 

○ A PC has received an urgent, desperate message from a contact within the Flaming Fist or even the Guild. A critical dispatch – detailing recent "Rot" outbreaks or strange cult activity – was supposed to be delivered but never arrived. The last known location of the courier was a grimy, forgotten district known to be hotly contested by several street gangs. The PCs arrive to find the district in chaos: not just gang violence, but signs of a deeper, more disturbing struggle – strange markings, unnaturally cold spots, and whispers of a 

"Whispering Hand" (a new street gang, secretly funded and influenced by House Aizen). 

4. The Noble's Whisper: 

○ A PC with a knack for information or a contact in higher circles is approached by a frantic, minor noble (perhaps from a family losing influence). They speak in hushed tones of a "chilling influence" that has spread through the Upper City. 

Their once-loyal servants are becoming withdrawn, displaying odd tics, and they've heard unsettling hymns in the dead of night. They believe a "new power" is seizing control, and they offer a desperate plea for the PCs to investigate. They'll hint that this influence seems to be coming from an organization with a strange, stylized "A" symbol. 



The Common Thread (Aizen's Ascendant Will & The Blackwoods' Corruption): 

● Pervasive "Rot": The corruption from the Blackwoods isn't just external; it's a creeping influence, affecting the city's infrastructure, people, and even the weather. NPCs might cough up black dust, or their eyes might occasionally glaze over. 

● The Aizen Influence: Aizen's mark, either the stylized "A" or a general sense of his charismatic, calculated presence, will be subtly present. His followers are impeccably dressed, unnervingly polite, and possess an almost fanatical devotion. They represent a "cleaner", more organized threat compared to the raw chaos of the Rot. 

● The Power Disparity: The PCs will encounter forces that demonstrate abilities beyond normal human understanding – the mutated resilience of Blackwoods creatures, the uncanny precision of Aizen's agents, or the desperate, raw power of the more fanatical street gangs. The PCs themselves, through their "Destiny" abilities, will be able to stand against this.

● Erosion of Order: The Flaming Fist is constantly on the back foot. Their resources are dwindling, and internal strife (often fomented by Aizen or the Guild) weakens them further. 



Initial Goal for the PCs: 

To survive the immediate threat and follow the most promising lead. They will quickly discover that the incidents are interconnected, part of a larger scheme to bring Baldur's Gate under a new, dark control. They'll have to rely on their burgeoning powers and the network of contacts they forge in a city teetering on the edge of oblivion. 

Introducing Aizen: 

Aizen should be a looming, almost mythical figure at first. His direct appearances should be rare and impactful. 

● The Unseen Hand: PCs will hear his name whispered with a mix of awe and terror. They'll see his agents operating with chilling efficiency, hinting at the vast resources and intelligence of House Aizen. 

● The Charismatic Leader: When he does appear, Aizen is impeccably dressed, disarmingly polite, and speaks with a calm, authoritative voice. He might offer the PCs a "choice" – to join his new order, to serve his vision for Baldur's Gate, or to be swept aside. He will subtly demonstrate a mastery over the "Darkness" or a similar fundamental power that chills the PCs to the bone. 

● The Temptation: Aizen might offer solutions to the city's suffering, promising order, security, and a future free from the "Rot" – but at a terrible price. He could even offer to "awaken" or enhance the PCs' own latent powers, but perhaps with a dark twist. 



The campaign will quickly escalate as the PCs uncover Aizen's true objectives – perhaps to merge Baldur's Gate with the corrupted Blackwoods, to harness the city's latent energies, or to use it as a stepping stone for an even grander, more terrifying plan. The street gangs will serve as a constant low-level threat and a source of desperate information, while the Blackwoods will be an ever-present, growing horror. The PCs' "Destiny" powers will be crucial in facing these threats, but they'll always be asking themselves: how much "Light" do we have left, and what will we become to survive this "Lightless Dawn"? 

Not the destiny rpg. The clone of dnd 5e 

Got it! My apologies for the misinterpretation. "Destiny" as a clone of D&D 5e suggests a D&D 5e-compatible system with similar core mechanics, but perhaps with unique lore, classes, or a specific setting. Since you're looking for Baldur's Gate and the existing factions, I'll assume "Destiny" means D&D 5e with specific thematic elements for a dark urban fantasy/horror feel, and potentially some custom rules for powers or resources that differentiate it slightly from standard D&D.

Let's rewind and build a robust Baldur's Gate campaign opening, incorporating The Blackwoods and Street Gangs, all within a D&D 5e framework, but with that 'Destiny-like' flavor you're hinting at – perhaps a sense of foreboding, a more grim world, and distinct character abilities or narrative beats. 



Campaign Title: The Shadow over the Gate: A Blighted Dawn 

System: Dungeons & Dragons 5th Edition (with a 'Destiny' overlay - implying a grimmer tone, possibly custom mechanics for 'influence' or 'corruption,' and focus on survival and urban intrigue). 

Setting: Baldur's Gate, Faerûn. A sprawling, grimy, yet historically rich metropolis. The city feels more oppressive and dangerous than usual, with a constant sense of unease. 

Core Themes: Urban decay, hidden horrors, moral compromises, the corrupting influence of power, survival against overwhelming odds, the search for hope in a dying city. 

Major Factions (D&D 5e focused, with darker undertones): 

● The Flaming Fist (City Watch): The overburdened, increasingly brutal, and often desperate military and police force. Grand Duke Ulder Ravengard struggles to maintain control. Corruption is rampant, not just from bribes, but from weariness and fear. They are spread thin, fighting on multiple fronts. 

● The Guild (Criminal Underworld): A vast, intricate network of thieves, smugglers, assassins, and racketeers. Divided into various crews and bosses, but often united against external threats. Their true power lies in their deep roots within the city's infrastructure and their ability to turn any crisis into profit. 

● The Knights of the Shield (The Veiled Hands): A highly secretive and influential cabal of wealthy merchants, nobles, and powerful mages. They believe they are the true guardians of Baldur's Gate, manipulating events from behind the scenes to maintain their vision of order, often clashing subtly with the Flaming Fist and Guild. They possess ancient knowledge and hidden resources. 

● Cult of the Dead Three (The Blight-Born): A horrifying, resurgent force. These cultists are utterly devoted to Bane, Bhaal, and Myrkul. Their rituals are gruesome, and their followers often exhibit unsettling resilience or minor, unnatural abilities. They seek to 

bring about a new age of suffering and death, preying on the city's despair. Bane's influence is particularly strong, manifesting as a drive for absolute control and crushing dominance. 

● House Aizen (The Ascendant Order): A relatively new, yet terrifyingly powerful and charismatic organization, led by Aizen. They have rapidly gained influence through shrewd investments, political maneuvering, and an unnerving ability to seemingly "fix"

problems that plague the city. Their true motives are veiled, but their growing power base and the unsettling devotion of their followers (often those who were desperate or disillusioned) are cause for alarm. Aizen himself is a master manipulator, capable of charming even the most hardened individuals. 

● The Blackwoods (The Blighted Expanse): Not just a forest, but a genuinely cursed and dangerous wilderness bordering Baldur's Gate. It's permeated by an ancient, malevolent energy that corrupts living things. Strange, mutated beasts, plant monsters, and even "blighted fey" dwell within. Periodically, these horrors spill out, forcing the Flaming Fist to dedicate valuable resources to patrols and containment. Rumors speak of ancient ruins and forgotten horrors within its depths. 

● Street Gangs (The Scrappers & The Claimants): Numerous, desperate, and often heavily armed groups of hardened survivors. Each gang controls specific blocks or districts, fighting viciously over resources, territory, and influence. They are distinct from the Guild, though often exploited by them. Some gangs might have discovered minor magical trinkets or adopted disturbing rituals that grant them a desperate edge. 

○ The Sallow Skins: Known for their distinctive, yellow-dyed scarves and their ruthless control of the Lower City's grimy market streets. 

○ The River Reavers: Operating from makeshift barges and hidden tunnels along the Chionthar, they specialize in smuggling and raiding riverside districts. ○ The Ash Dogs: A particularly brutal gang from the Outer City slums, known for their feral nature and reliance on improvised weapons and fire. They are dangerously unpredictable. 



Starting Point: A City on the Brink 

The campaign opens during a period of escalating tension in Baldur's Gate. The summer heat is oppressive, but it's not just the weather; there's a palpable sense of unease, a gnawing dread that something terrible is brewing. Whispers of strange illnesses, unexplained disappearances, and escalating cult activity fill the taverns. 

The Hook (choose one or combine elements for the PCs): 

The Player Characters (PCs) are individuals with their own reasons for being in Baldur's Gate. They could be adventurers seeking coin, disillusioned former soldiers, Guild members looking for a score, or even Flaming Fist officers trying to make a difference. They should have some connection to the city. 

1. The Dockside Massacre: 

○ One or more PCs are near the Gray Harbor, perhaps looking for work, delivering goods, or simply passing through. Suddenly, the chaotic peace is shattered. A small group of Flaming Fist guards are ambushed by a surprisingly 

well-organized group of River Reavers, but this isn't just a gang fight. The Reavers are backed by several unnaturally strong, gaunt figures bearing the chilling symbol of Bane. The fight is brutal, ending with a massacre. The cultists

are searching for a specific crate amongst the cargo – something pulsating faintly with dark magic. The PCs are eyewitnesses, or caught in the crossfire. 

2. The Blighted Outbreak: 

○ A PC is in a poorer district of the Lower City, perhaps the Outer City slums, dealing with an NPC contact or exploring. Suddenly, screams erupt. A wave of monstrous, mutated creatures – perhaps giant, blight-ridden rats or grotesque, walking fungal growths – bursts from a sewage grate or a forgotten alley. They seem to be driven by some unseen force, attacking indiscriminately. A few desperate members of the Ash Dogs gang are trying to fight them off, but they are clearly overwhelmed. The creatures have strange, almost crystalline growths on their bodies, reminiscent of corrupted flora from the Blackwoods. 

3. The Noble's Desperate Plea: 

○ A PC with connections to the Upper City or a reputation for problem-solving is approached by a frantic, minor noble of the House Vanthampur (perhaps a lesser-known cousin, not tied to the main plot of Descent into Avernus). Their younger sibling has gone missing after associating with a new, seemingly benevolent "philanthropic society" that has sprung up recently. The noble is too terrified to go to the Flaming Fist directly, as they believe this "society" (secretly House Aizen) has spies everywhere. They offer a significant sum for discretion and recovery of their sibling. 

4. The Flaming Fist's Strain: 

○ A PC is a member of the Flaming Fist. Their patrol route has become increasingly dangerous. They are tasked with investigating a series of 

increasingly bizarre reports: a prominent Guild leader found ritually 

dismembered, several members of the Sallow Skins gang mysteriously 

vanishing, and a general increase in a palpable, oppressive fear in the streets. Their superior, Captain Zodge (or a new, equally stressed captain), is clearly at his wit's end and assigns the PCs to investigate the growing unrest, hinting that something far worse than common crime is at play. 



The Common Thread (Aizen's Web & The Blackwoods' Spread): 

Regardless of the starting scenario, the PCs will notice subtle similarities: 

● Unnatural Strength/Resilience: The antagonists (cultists, mutated creatures, certain gang members) demonstrate a disturbing level of power or an unsettling ability to shrug off wounds. This isn't just brute force; it feels wrong, almost unnatural. 

● The Cult's Spreading Influence: The symbol of Bane (a black hand or fist) or other Dead Three iconography will appear in unexpected places, often crudely carved or painted. The cult seems to be recruiting from the desperate and the disillusioned among the street gangs and common folk. 

● Aizen's Elegant Touch: Aizen's agents, whether directly involved or indirectly benefiting, are always impeccably dressed, well-spoken, and seem to know more than they let on. They move with an unsettling grace and confidence, often cleaning up

messes or appearing just after major incidents. His symbol (a stylized, elegant 'A') might be found on documents or subtle trinkets. 

● The Blackwoods' encroachment: The air occasionally carries a faint, sickly sweet scent of decay, even far from the woods. Reports of strange growths on buildings or unusual animal behavior increase. The city feels like it's slowly being suffocated from within and without. 

● Rising Paranoia: NPCs are more suspicious, less willing to talk, and quick to flee. The Flaming Fist seems overwhelmed, and the Guild is more aggressive, consolidating power. 



Initial Goal for the PCs: 

To investigate the immediate incident and survive the escalating threats. As they do, they will begin to uncover the connections between the seemingly disparate events. They'll find that the cult activity, the Blackwoods' corruption, and Aizen's rising influence are all threads in a terrifying tapestry designed to seize control of Baldur's Gate. 

Introducing Aizen: 

Aizen should be a master of subtlety and manipulation. His initial presence is felt through his actions and his devoted followers. 

● The Enigmatic Philanthropist: He might first appear as a public figure, a new benevolent force in the city who is funding repairs, providing aid to the poor, or establishing new institutions. He will be charismatic, articulate, and disarmingly charming, perhaps even offering the PCs a tempting proposition or valuable information, seemingly out of the goodness of his heart. 

● The Information Broker: The PCs might find themselves needing information that only a powerful individual like Aizen could possess. He might grant an audience, subtly probing their allegiances and offering a glimpse of his vast influence and resources, always with a hidden agenda. 

● The Unseen Puppet Master: His agents will be everywhere, operating with chilling efficiency. PCs will hear whispers of "House Aizen" being behind various successful endeavors or the convenient removal of obstacles, always just beyond the PCs' direct reach initially. 



The campaign will quickly propel the PCs into the heart of Baldur's Gate's burgeoning crisis. They will have to navigate the shifting allegiances of the Flaming Fist, the Guild, the Knights of the Shield, and the desperate street gangs, all while facing the creeping horror of the Cult of the Dead Three and the monstrous threats from the Blackwoods. Ultimately, they will discover that Aizen is at the center of it all, orchestrating a grand design for Baldur's Gate, and his true power and ambition will test the PCs' limits and their own moral compass. 

Stop giving me future plot details and spoilers. Start the campaign

Got it. My apologies. No more future plot details or spoilers. We'll start right now, in the thick of it, with the immediate situation. 



Here's the beginning of your D&D 5th Edition campaign in Baldur's Gate, with all the elements you've asked for, but focusing solely on the present moment. 



The Shadow over the Gate: A Blighted Dawn 

Setting: Baldur's Gate. The air is thick with the scent of damp stone, the distant cries of gulls, and the ever-present, low hum of a city that never truly sleeps. It's late evening, the sun having dipped below the horizon, painting the western sky in hues of deep orange and bruised purple. The Lower City, where you find yourselves, is a labyrinth of winding streets, close-packed buildings, and the occasional burst of raucous laughter or argument from a tavern. 

The past few weeks have been unsettling. The usual street arguments have grown sharper, more violent. Whispers of a creeping sickness in the Outer City are circulating, and the Flaming Fist patrols seem more grim-faced and frequent. Even the gulls seem louder, more frantic. 



Choose ONE of the following starting scenarios for your character(s). When you choose, tell me which character (Player Character, or PC) you are using for this scenario if you have multiple in mind. If you are playing solo, just state your character's name. 

Scenario 1: The Dockside Massacre 

You are near the Gray Harbor, perhaps checking on a contact, watching the last ships unload, or simply cutting through to reach another part of the city. The air is thick with the smell of brine and fish. The lanterns on the docks cast flickering pools of light, leaving deep shadows between the stacked crates and coiled ropes. 

Suddenly, a cacophony erupts from further down the main dock: shouts, the clang of steel, and then a chilling scream. It's coming from a section where a small Flaming Fist patrol was overseeing the unloading of a modest cargo vessel – the Sea Serpent. 

You hear a Flaming Fist officer's desperate cry: "Ambush! Blades! They're... unnatural!" What do you do? 

Scenario 2: The Blighted Outbreak

You find yourself in the grimy, forgotten district of Tanshire, on the western edge of the Lower City, bordering the Outer City slums. It's a place of cramped tenements, overflowing refuse, and the faint, sweet-sickly odor of decay that's been more noticeable lately. You might be here looking for a specific item, meeting a shady contact, or perhaps you simply live in this neglected part of the city. 



A sudden, piercing shriek cuts through the evening air, followed by terrified shouts. It's coming from a narrow alleyway just ahead of you. Then, a chilling scuttling sound, too large for rats, too fast for anything common. The shouts turn to desperate cries for help. 

A figure, covered in filth and blood, stumbles out of the alley, eyes wide with terror, before collapsing. Behind them, you glimpse movement in the shadows: something large, hunched, and moving with an unnatural, skittering gait. 

What do you do? 



Scenario 3: The Noble's Desperate Plea 

You are in a slightly more respectable district, perhaps near the bustling streets leading to the Upper City, or perhaps even in a quiet, secluded common area. You've been approached (or sought out) by Lady Anina Vanthampur, a distant, lesser-known cousin of the main Vanthampur ducal line. She's dressed in fine, though slightly disheveled, clothes, and her face is etched with profound worry. 

She pulls you aside, her voice a desperate whisper, almost too quiet to hear over the distant city din. "Please," she pleads, her eyes darting around nervously. "My younger brother, Lord Theon, he... he's gone. He became involved with this 'Benevolent Aid Society' that opened recently. They seemed so... helpful. But now, he hasn't returned. I fear... I fear the worst. I cannot go to the Flaming Fist directly. I believe they have eyes everywhere. Please, adventurer, can you help me? Find him. Discreetly. I will pay... handsomely." 

She presses a small, velvet pouch into your hand. It feels heavy with coin. She gestures subtly towards a newly established building a few blocks away – a large, recently renovated townhouse displaying a new, elegant sign: "The Ascendant Aid Society." 

What do you do? 



Scenario 4: The Flaming Fist's Strain 

You are a member of the Flaming Fist, currently on patrol in the district of Bloomridge, a working-class area known for its breweries and merchant houses. The air is thick with the smell of hops and spilled ale. Your superior, Captain Zodge, a grizzled veteran with a perpetually

grim expression, walks beside you, his hand never far from his sword hilt. He's been on edge for weeks. 



"Another one, damn it all," Zodge mutters, looking at a recently boarded-up storefront. "This makes five businesses hit in as many nights, and three Guild members gone missing without a trace. And the stench... it's getting worse, isn't it?" He gestures vaguely towards the distant Outer City. "This isn't typical Guild mischief. Too clean. Too... quiet. I need eyes on the ground, someone who can cut through the usual B.S. and find out what's really happening." 

He turns to you, his eyes hard. "I'm pulling you from the main patrol. I want you to go to the Dye Works Alley. A particularly nasty group of Sallow Skins were reported there earlier, causing a ruckus. But it's not the Sallow Skins I'm worried about. Just... keep your wits about you. Something's rotten in this city, and it ain't just the sewers." He gives you a grim nod and hands you a small, rolled-up sketch of a crude, unfamiliar symbol. "If you see this... be careful." 

What do you do?

Campaign Title: The Shadow over the Gate: A Blighted Dawn 

System: Dungeons & Dragons 5th Edition 

Setting: Baldur's Gate. The city feels more strained than usual. The oppressive heat of summer has done little to dissipate the growing tension. Whispers of strange phenomena – unusual animal behavior, odd illnesses, and cult activity – ripple through the crowded streets. The familiar scent of the harbor, the breweries, and the city's general grime is now frequently tinged with something else: a subtle, sickly sweetness, like decay. 

Aizen, your personal details are now fully integrated: 

● Name: Aizen 

● Race: Human 

● Class: Wizard (Level 1, Bladesinger specialization will come later) 

● Background: Sage 

● Alignment: Neutral Evil 

● HP: 8 

● Proficiency Bonus: +2 

● Saving Throws: Intelligence, Wisdom 

● Skills: Arcana +6, History +4, Insight +3, Persuasion +5 (assuming standard attribute application with proficiency) 

● Attributes: 

○ Strength: 10 

○ Dexterity: 16 

○ Constitution: 14 

○ Intelligence: 18 

○ Wisdom: 12 

○ Charisma: 16 

● Spells Known: 

○ Cantrips: Fire Bolt, Prestidigitation 

○ 1st Level Spells: Shield, Absorb Elements, Mage Armor, Magic Missile ● Equipment: Quarterstaff, Component Pouch, Scholar's Pack, Spellbook, Dagger, Common Clothes (subtly refined), Belt Pouch with 15 gp.

The Immediate Situation: Choose Your Starting Point 



A new dawn breaks, but it feels blighted. The city of Baldur's Gate is a powder keg. Choose one of the following scenarios to begin Aizen's journey in this fresh campaign. 



Scenario 1: The Dockside Uprising 

It's just after dusk at the Gray Harbor. The usual din of longshoremen and merchants is giving way to the quieter hum of the evening. You, Aizen, are perhaps observing the flow of illicit goods, testing the reach of your own influence, or simply seeking a discreet route through the bustling docks. 

Suddenly, a commotion erupts near Pier 3. The sounds of a heated argument escalate into shouts, then the unmistakable clang of steel. A contingent of River Reavers, a ruthless street gang specializing in dockside smuggling and violence, are clashing not with the Flaming Fist, but with a smaller, more disciplined group of figures wearing dark, hooded cloaks. These cloaked figures move with an unnerving efficiency, their blades glinting in the lantern light. One of the Reavers cries out, "They're not just some new gang! They're... dead!" 

You are close enough to witness the skirmish without being immediately drawn in, but the chaos is spreading. 

What does Aizen do? 



Scenario 2: The Blighted Slum 

You are in the grimy, neglected district of Tanshire, on the western edge of the Lower City, an area perpetually overshadowed by the wealthy Upper City. You might be here gathering information, making contact with a desperate informant, or observing the plight of the common folk for your own purposes. The air here usually carries the scent of refuse and dampness, but tonight there's a new, sickly-sweet tang, like overripe fruit mixed with decay. 

Suddenly, a piercing shriek tears through the relative quiet, followed by terrified shouts. It's coming from a cramped alleyway just ahead. Then, a chilling, chitinous scuttling sound, too large and too quick to be normal vermin. A figure, covered in filth and spattered blood, stumbles out of the alley, eyes wide with horror, before collapsing into a twitching heap. Behind them, from the alley's shadows, you glimpse something moving: large, hunched forms, their skin mottled and their movements unnaturally fast, amidst the desperate cries of a few Ash Dogs gang members who are clearly outmatched.

The stench of decay intensifies. 



What does Aizen do? 



Scenario 3: The Whispering Society 

You are in a more affluent, but still somewhat discreet, part of the Lower City. You've been invited (or perhaps orchestrated an invitation for yourself) to a reception at the recently established Ascendant Aid Society – a new organization that has swiftly gained popularity among the city's desperate and ambitious alike, thanks to its seemingly boundless charity and influential connections. The Society's elegant townhouse is abuzz with the murmurs of hopeful supplicants and well-dressed agents. 

As you mingle, an unassuming but well-dressed merchant approaches you, his face pale and eyes darting nervously. He lowers his voice to a whisper. "Forgive my intrusion, honored guest," he begins, "but you... you have an air of understanding. My daughter... she joined the Society last week. Full of hope. But when she visited me yesterday, she seemed different. Her eyes... a strange light. And she spoke of 'the Voice of Truth' that only the Society understands. I fear she is ensnared. I saw her speak to one of them," he gestures subtly to a group of Society agents across the room, who carry themselves with an almost unnatural serenity. "They wore a small, ornate 'A' pin. Please, sir, I beg you... is there something sinister here?" 

The merchant's desperation is palpable. A few of the Society agents glance towards him, their serene expressions not quite reaching their eyes. 

What does Aizen do? 

dm mode: change my class to paladin 

Understood. DM mode engaged. 

Aizen, your class is now Paladin. 

Here's your updated character sheet, integrating the Paladin class at Level 1, while retaining your established attributes and background, and making adjustments for starting equipment and proficiencies. 

Aizen 

● Race: Human 

● Class: Paladin (Level 1) 

● Background: Sage (This is an interesting combination for a Paladin, suggesting a scholarly pursuit of a specific Oath or a darker, more philosophical approach to their power.)

● Alignment: Neutral Evil (This is a very unusual and intriguing alignment for a Paladin, as the class typically aligns with good. This implies Aizen might be a Paladin of a darker deity, an oathbreaker, or serving a very specific, self-serving creed that grants him divine power. This will be an exciting element to explore!) 

● HP: 10 (1d10 + Con mod) 

● Proficiency Bonus: +2 

● Saving Throws: Wisdom, Charisma 

● Skills: Arcana +6, History +4, Insight +3, Persuasion +5 (Retaining Sage skills, gaining two from Paladin list. Let me know if you'd like to swap any Paladin proficiencies for others!) 

● Attributes: 

○ Strength: 10 

○ Dexterity: 16 

○ Constitution: 14 

○ Intelligence: 18 

○ Wisdom: 12 

○ Charisma: 16 

● Paladin Features (Level 1): 

○ Divine Sense: As an action, you can open your awareness to detect evil and celestial, fiend, or undead creatures within 60 feet. You can use this a number of times equal to 1 + your Charisma modifier (min 1). 

○ Lay on Hands: As an action, you can touch a creature and draw power from a pool of healing, restoring 5 hit points (Paladin level x 5). Alternatively, you can expend 5 hit points from your pool to cure one disease or neutralize one poison on a creature. 

● Proficiencies: 

○ Armor: Light armor, medium armor, heavy armor, shields 

○ Weapons: Simple weapons, martial weapons 

○ Tools: None 

● Equipment (Updated for Paladin): 

○ Chain Mail: (AC 16) - This replaces your common clothes and provides significant protection. 

○ Shield: (AC +2, total AC 18 with Chain Mail) 

○ Quarterstaff: (1d6 bludgeoning, versatile 1d8) - You can still use this, but you also have proficiencies for martial weapons. 

○ Five Javelins: (1d6 piercing, thrown, range 30/120) 

○ Priest's Pack: (Contains a backpack, a blanket, 10 candles, a tinderbox, an alms box, 2 blocks of incense, a censer, vestments, 2 days of rations, and a waterskin.) 

○ Holy Symbol: (Appropriate to the source of your dark power or your specific Oath – we can define this later, or you can suggest it now!) 

○ Belt Pouch with 15 gp.

Now, Aizen, a Paladin of formidable (and potentially dark) power, what does your character do in the chosen scenario? 



Scenario 1: The Dockside Uprising 

It's just after dusk at the Gray Harbor. The usual din is shattered by shouts and the clang of steel near Pier 3. River Reavers are clashing with a smaller, more disciplined group of dark-cloaked figures. One Reaver cries out, "They're not just some new gang! They're... dead!" You are close enough to witness the skirmish without being immediately drawn in, but the chaos is spreading. 

What does Aizen do? 

Sources 

can you see the child of bane special bonuses? 

Show thinking 

What are the specific abilities or bonuses associated with being a "Child of Bane" in D&D 5e? Are there any established homebrew or official D&D 5e archetypes or lineages related to Bane that grant special bonuses? Child of Bane D&D 5e special bonuses Bane D&D 5e lineage abilities D&D 5e offspring of Bane features You're asking about special bonuses for being a "Child of Bane" in D&D 5th Edition. 

Based on official D&D 5e rules and common lore, there isn't a pre-defined "Child of Bane" racial or lineage template that grants specific, codified bonuses like a standard race (e.g., a Tiefling's infernal legacy). 

However, in a D&D campaign, especially one focusing on divine influence and darker powers like Bane's, a DM could certainly implement such features as homebrew abilities to reflect Aizen's unique connection to the Lord of Tyranny. 

Looking at the search results, while there isn't a direct "Child of Bane" stat block, we see concepts like: 

● "Banedead" and "Baneguard" are undead creatures created by Bane's clerics, possessing abilities related to fear, martial prowess, and sometimes innate spellcasting (often cleric spells like bane itself, or even magic missile). 

● "Black Gauntlet of Bane" are powerful human followers who gain immunities (like frightened), an Aura of Terror (disadvantage on attack rolls/saves for hostile creatures nearby), and martial/cleric spellcasting. 

● The god Bane's stat block includes abilities like Fear Aura, Conquering Presence, Aura of Conquest, and Hateful Rebuke, all reflecting his tyrannical and oppressive nature.

If we were to incorporate "Child of Bane" special bonuses for Aizen, given his Paladin class and Neutral Evil alignment, we could consider traits that reflect Bane's portfolio of tyranny, fear, order through oppression, and martial dominance. 



Here are some potential homebrew bonuses for a "Child of Bane" at Level 1 in D&D 5e, which we can discuss and decide if you'd like to add any to Aizen: 

(Choose one or two, or we can tailor something else. Remember, these would be custom additions beyond standard Paladin features) 

1. Tyrant's Gaze (Limited): As a bonus action, you can attempt to instill fear. One creature you can see within 30 feet that can hear or see you must succeed on a Wisdom saving throw (DC 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier) or be frightened of you until the end of its next turn. Once you use this feature, you can't use it again until you finish a short or long rest. 

○ Reflects Bane's fear-inducing presence. 

2. Unwavering Discipline: You have advantage on saving throws against being frightened. 

○ Reflects Bane's emphasis on absolute control and mental fortitude. 

3. Aura of Minor Command: When a hostile creature starts its turn within 5 feet of you, you can use your reaction to impose disadvantage on one attack roll it makes against you before the end of its turn. You can use this a number of times equal to your Charisma modifier (minimum of once) per long rest. This effect expands to 10 feet at Paladin Level 6. 

○ A very minor, early version of Bane's aura, focusing on control. 

4. Mark of the Black Hand: You gain proficiency in the Intimidation skill. If you are already proficient in Intimidation, you add double your proficiency bonus to checks you make with it. 

○ Reflects the direct connection to Bane's symbols and methods. 

5. Minor Resilience: You have resistance to necrotic damage. (This might be too strong for Level 1, but depends on the campaign's power level.) 

○ A nod to the toughness seen in some of Bane's undead servants. 



So, Aizen, do you recall if there were any specific "Child of Bane" bonuses you had in mind from the previous chat, or would you like to consider adding one (or more, if minor) of the above homebrew options to reflect your connection to Bane? 

Once we settle on this, we'll return to your chosen starting scenario: Scenario 1: The Dockside Uprising.

The Shadow over the Gate: A Blighted Dawn System: Dungeons & Dragons 5th Edition (with "Destiny Rulebook" overrides) 

Setting: Baldur's Gate. The city feels more strained than usual. The oppressive heat of summer has done little to dissipate the growing tension. Whispers of strange phenomena – unusual animal behavior, odd illnesses, and cult activity – ripple through the crowded streets. The familiar scent of the harbor, the breweries, and the city's general grime is now frequently tinged with something else: a subtle, sickly sweetness, like decay. 



Aizen: The Ascendant Paladin 

● Race: Human 

● Class: Paladin (Level 1) 

● Background: Sage 

● Alignment: Neutral Evil 

● Heritage: Child of Bane 

● Hit Points (HP): 10 (1d10 + Con mod) 

● Proficiency Bonus: +2 

● Attributes: (Base + Child of Bane bonus + Legendary Item bonus) 

○ Strength: 10 (+1 from Holy Armor of Radiance) = 11 

○ Dexterity: 16 

○ Constitution: 14 (+2 from Child of Bane +1 from Holy Armor of Radiance) = 17 ○ Intelligence: 18 (+2 from Child of Bane) = 20 

○ Wisdom: 12 (+2 from Child of Bane) = 14

○ Charisma: 16 (+2 from Child of Bane +1 from Holy Armor of Radiance) = 19 ● Note: The Child of Bane bonus raises scores above 20 to a hard cap of 22. ● Saving Throws: Wisdom +5, Charisma +6 (Proficiency + Ability Modifier) ● Skills: Arcana +7, History +5, Insight +4, Persuasion +6 (Proficiency + Ability Modifier) ● Effective Defense (ED): 20 (Higher of DR and AR) 

○ Dodge Rating (DR) Score: 11 (10 + Dexterity Modifier (3)) 

○ Armor Rating (AR) Score: 20 (10 + Armor Bonus (+4 from Holy Armor) + Shield Bonus (+3 from Bulwark) + Half Strength Modifier (0 - not in heavy armor)) ● Damage Mitigation: 

○ Armor Mitigation: 10 (Armor Bonus (+4) + Shield Bonus (+3) + Half Strength Modifier (0)) 

■ Reduces all incoming physical damage (slashing, piercing, bludgeoning) from attacks that hit your ED by this value. 

○ Dodge Mitigation: +3 (Equal to your full Dexterity Modifier) 

■ Reduces all incoming damage from effects that require a Dexterity Saving Throw (e.g., Fireball, dragon's breath, traps) by this value. 

● Paladin Features (Level 1): 

○ Divine Sense: As an action, you can open your awareness to detect evil and celestial, fiend, or undead creatures within 60 feet. You can use this 1 + your Charisma modifier (5) = 6 times per long rest. 

○ Lay on Hands: As an action, you can touch a creature and draw power from a pool of healing, restoring 5 hit points. Alternatively, you can expend 5 hit points from your pool to cure one disease or neutralize one poison on a creature. ● Proficiencies: 

○ Armor: Light armor, medium armor, heavy armor, shields 

○ Weapons: Simple weapons, martial weapons 

○ Tools: None 

● Equipment: 

○ +4 Holy Armor of Radiance: Provides +4 to AR calculations. Grants radiant resistance and +1 to STR/CON/CHA. (Current AC 18 with shield). 

○ Sunderer of Souls (+4 Holy Avenger Greatsword): Custom damage (3d6+8 slashing + 4d6 radiant) and crit rules (x3 multiplier). 

○ +3 Bulwark of the Righteous (Shield): Provides +3 to AR calculations. Grants advantage on saves vs. fear. 

○ Cloak of Resistance: Grants Advantage on all saving throws against spells and magical effects. 

○ Quarterstaff: (1d6 bludgeoning, versatile 1d8) 

○ Component Pouch 

○ Scholar's Pack 

○ Spellbook (Despite being a Paladin, your Sage background and high INT might imply this is a general research journal or for your old wizard studies, or perhaps it's a specific, custom item given your unique nature. We'll interpret this as a valuable personal item for now.) 

○ Dagger

○ Common Clothes: (Assumed worn beneath the armor, or perhaps for casual moments.) 

○ Belt Pouch with 15 gp. 

○ Five Javelins 

○ Priest's Pack (Contains a backpack, a blanket, 10 candles, a tinderbox, an alms box, 2 blocks of incense, a censer, vestments, 2 days of rations, and a 

waterskin.) 

○ Holy Symbol: (Given your Neutral Evil Paladin status, this is likely a symbol of Bane, or a custom symbol representing your unique, oppressive Oath.) 



The Immediate Situation: Choose Your Starting Point 

A new dawn breaks, but it feels blighted. The city of Baldur's Gate is a powder keg. Choose one of the following scenarios to begin Aizen's journey in this fresh campaign. 



Scenario 1: The Dockside Uprising 

It's just after dusk at the Gray Harbor. The usual din of longshoremen and merchants is giving way to the quieter hum of the evening. You, Aizen, are perhaps observing the flow of illicit goods, testing the reach of your own influence, or simply seeking a discreet route through the bustling docks. 

Suddenly, a commotion erupts near Pier 3. The sounds of a heated argument escalate into shouts, then the unmistakable clang of steel. A contingent of River Reavers, a ruthless street gang specializing in dockside smuggling and violence, are clashing not with the Flaming Fist, but with a smaller, more disciplined group of figures wearing dark, hooded cloaks. These cloaked figures move with an unnerving efficiency, their blades glinting in the lantern light. One of the Reavers cries out, "They're not just some new gang! They're... dead!" 

You are close enough to witness the skirmish without being immediately drawn in, but the chaos is spreading. 

What does Aizen do? 



Scenario 2: The Blighted Slum 

You are in the grimy, neglected district of Tanshire, on the western edge of the Lower City, an area perpetually overshadowed by the wealthy Upper City. You might be here gathering information, making contact with a desperate informant, or observing the plight of the common

folk for your own purposes. The air here usually carries the scent of refuse and dampness, but tonight there's a new, sickly-sweet tang, like overripe fruit mixed with decay. 



Suddenly, a piercing shriek tears through the relative quiet, followed by terrified shouts. It's coming from a cramped alleyway just ahead. Then, a chilling, chitinous scuttling sound, too large and too quick to be normal vermin. A figure, covered in filth and spattered blood, stumbles out of the alley, eyes wide with horror, before collapsing into a twitching heap. Behind them, from the alley's shadows, you glimpse something moving: large, hunched forms, their skin mottled and their movements unnaturally fast, amidst the desperate cries of a few Ash Dogs gang members who are clearly outmatched. 

The stench of decay intensifies. 

What does Aizen do? 



Scenario 3: The Whispering Society 

You are in a more affluent, but still somewhat discreet, part of the Lower City. You've been invited (or perhaps orchestrated an invitation for yourself) to a reception at the recently established Ascendant Aid Society – a new organization that has swiftly gained popularity among the city's desperate and ambitious alike, thanks to its seemingly boundless charity and influential connections. The Society's elegant townhouse is abuzz with the murmurs of hopeful supplicants and well-dressed agents. 

As you mingle, an unassuming but well-dressed merchant approaches you, his face pale and eyes darting nervously. He lowers his voice to a whisper. "Forgive my intrusion, honored guest," he begins, "but you... you have an air of understanding. My daughter... she joined the Society last week. Full of hope. But when she visited me yesterday, she seemed different. Her eyes... a strange light. And she spoke of 'the Voice of Truth' that only the Society understands. I fear she is ensnared. I saw her speak to one of them," he gestures subtly to a group of Society agents across the room, who carry themselves with an almost unnatural serenity. "They wore a small, ornate 'A' pin. Please, sir, I beg you... is there something sinister here?" 

The merchant's desperation is palpable. A few of the Society agents glance towards him, their serene expressions not quite reaching their eyes. 

What does Aizen do?

Aizen: The Ascendant Paladin of Whispers 

● Race: Human

● Proficiency Bonus: +3 

● Hit Points (HP): 

○ Paladin 1: 1d10 + Con (10 + 3 = 13) 

○ Paladin 2: 1d10 + Con (Avg 5.5 + 3 = 8.5) 

○ Bard 3-8: 6d8 + Con (Avg 4.5 * 6 + 3 * 6 = 27 + 18 = 45) 

○ Total HP: 13 + 8 + 45 = 66 HP 

● Attributes: (Base + Child of Bane bonus + Legendary Item bonus) 

○ Strength: 10 (+1 from Holy Armor of Radiance) = 11 

○ Dexterity: 16 

○ Constitution: 14 (+2 from Child of Bane +1 from Holy Armor of Radiance) = 17 ○ Intelligence: 18 (+2 from Child of Bane) = 20

○ Charisma: 16 (+2 from Child of Bane +1 from Holy Armor of Radiance) = 19 ● Saving Throws: 

○ Paladin: Wisdom, Charisma 

○ Bard: Dexterity, Charisma 

○ Proficient: Dexterity +6, Wisdom +5, Charisma +9 (Proficiency + Ability Modifier) ● Skills: (From Sage background and Paladin/Bard choices - 3 from Bard at Level 1, 1 from Paladin at Level 1. Plus expertise) 

○ Arcana +9 (Proficiency + Int Mod, Expertise if chosen) 

○ History +7 (Proficiency + Int Mod, Expertise if chosen)

■ Expertise (Bard 3): Choose 2 of your proficient skills. Let's assume Arcana and Persuasion, as they fit your concept of a manipulative, 

knowledgeable individual. Or we can choose others for different flavors (e.g., Deception and Intimidation). Let me know your preference! 

■ Assuming Expertise in Arcana and Persuasion: 

■ Arcana: 20 (Int 20 + Prof +3 + Exp +3) => becomes Arcana +9 

(Int +5 + Prof +3 + Exp +3) 

■ Persuasion: 19 (Cha 19 + Prof +3 + Exp +3) => becomes 

Persuasion +9 (Cha +4 + Prof +3 + Exp +3) 

● Effective Defense (ED): 20 (Higher of DR and AR) 

○ Dodge Rating (DR) Score: 11 (10 + Dexterity Modifier (3)) 

○ Armor Rating (AR) Score: 20 (10 + Armor Bonus (+4 from Holy Armor) + Shield Bonus (+3 from Bulwark) + Half Strength Modifier (0 - not in heavy armor)) ● Damage Mitigation: 

○ Armor Mitigation: 10 (Armor Bonus (+4) + Shield Bonus (+3) + Half Strength Modifier (0)) 

■ Reduces all incoming physical damage (slashing, piercing, bludgeoning) from attacks that hit your ED by this value. 

○ Dodge Mitigation: +3 (Equal to your full Dexterity Modifier) 

■ Reduces all incoming damage from effects that require a Dexterity Saving Throw (e.g., Fireball, dragon's breath, traps) by this value. 

● Paladin Features (Level 2): 

○ Divine Sense: As an action, you can open your awareness to detect evil and celestial, fiend, or undead creatures within 60 feet. You can use this 1 + your Charisma modifier (1+4=5) = 5 times per long rest. 

○ Lay on Hands: As an action, you can touch a creature and draw power from a pool of healing, restoring 10 hit points (Paladin level x 5). Alternatively, you can expend 5 hit points from your pool to cure one disease or neutralize one poison on a creature. 

○ Fighting Style: Choose one. Given your Dexterity and Bladesinger background, or the heavy armor, which do you prefer? 

■ Defense: +1 AC while wearing armor. (Making your AC 19 with shield). ■ Dueling: +2 damage when wielding a single melee weapon in one hand and no other weapons. 

■ Great Weapon Fighting: Reroll 1s or 2s on damage dice for two-handed weapons. 

■ Protection: Use reaction to impose disadvantage on attack against ally near you with shield. 

○ Spellcasting: You can cast Paladin spells. (See below for spell slots). ○ Divine Smite: When you hit a creature with a melee weapon attack, you can expend one paladin spell slot to deal radiant damage to the target, in addition to the weapon's damage. (1st level: 2d8, +1d8 for undead/fiend).

● Bard Features (Level 6): 

○ Spellcasting: You can cast Bard spells. (See below for spell slots). ○ Bardic Inspiration (d8): As a bonus action, give a creature (other than yourself) within 60 feet a Bardic Inspiration die. They can add it to one ability check, attack roll, or saving throw within 10 minutes. You have a number of uses equal to your Charisma modifier (4), regaining all on a long rest. 

○ Jack of All Trades: Add half your proficiency bonus (rounded down) to any ability check you make that doesn't already include your proficiency bonus. ○ Song of Rest: If you or any friendly creatures who can hear your performance regain hit points at the end of a short rest, each of those creatures regains an extra 1d6 hit points. 

○ Bard College (Bard 3): Choose a Bard College. Given your theme, consider: ■ College of Lore: Bonus proficiencies, Cutting Words (use Bardic 

Inspiration to reduce attack roll/ability check/damage roll). 

■ College of Valor: Combat Inspiration (use Bardic Inspiration for damage or AC), proficiency with medium armor, shields, and martial weapons. 

(You already have armor/weapon proficiencies from Paladin). 

■ College of Swords: Dueling fighting style, Blade Flourish (use Bardic Inspiration on attack for bonus damage/effect). 

■ College of Glamour: Mantle of Inspiration (temporary HP and 

movement), Enthralling Performance. 

■ College of Whispers: Psychic Blades (bonus psychic damage), Words of Terror (cause fear). This seems highly fitting for a Neutral Evil Paladin and Child of Bane. 

■ Let's assume College of Whispers for now, given your alignment and background. 

■ Psychic Blades (Bard 3): When you hit a creature with a weapon attack, you can expend one use of Bardic Inspiration to deal 3d6 

psychic damage to the target. You can do this only once per 

round. 

■ Words of Terror (Bard 3): As an action, you can frighten a 

creature for 1 hour. Requires a Charisma (Deception) check 

contested by Wisdom (Insight). Can only be used on creatures 

that can understand you and are not hostile. 

■ Mantle of Whispers (Bard 6): When a humanoid dies within 30 

feet of you, you can use your reaction to magically capture its 

shadow. For 1 hour, you become its perfect disguise, including 

speech. This is a very powerful infiltration tool. 

○ Countercharm (Bard 6): As an action, you can start a performance that lasts until the end of your next turn. During that time, you and any friendly creatures within 30 feet of you have advantage on saving throws against being frightened or charmed. 

● Proficiencies: 

○ Armor: Light armor, medium armor, heavy armor, shields

○ Weapons: Simple weapons, martial weapons 

○ Tools: None 

● Spellcasting: Aizen casts Paladin spells (Charisma-based) and Bard spells (Charisma-based). 

○ Spell Slots (Full Caster Level 7: Bard 6 + Paladin 2/2 = 7): 

■ 1st Level: 4 slots 

■ 2nd Level: 3 slots 

■ 3rd Level: 3 slots 

■ 4th Level: 1 slot 

○ Spells Known (Bard): 9 Bard spells known (from any level you have slots for). Can be chosen later based on your preference. 

○ Spells Prepared (Paladin): Charisma modifier (4) + half Paladin level (1) = 5 Paladin spells. Can be chosen later. 

○ Spell Save DC: 8 + Proficiency Bonus (3) + Charisma Modifier (4) = 15 ○ Spell Attack Modifier: Proficiency Bonus (3) + Charisma Modifier (4) = +7 ● Legendary Equipment: 

○ +4 Holy Armor of Radiance: Provides +4 to AR calculations. Grants radiant resistance and +1 to STR/CON/CHA. (Current AC 18 with shield). 

○ Sunderer of Souls (+4 Holy Avenger Greatsword): Custom damage (3d6+8 slashing + 4d6 radiant) and crit rules (x3 multiplier). 

○ +3 Bulwark of the Righteous (Shield): Provides +3 to AR calculations. Grants advantage on saves vs. fear. 

○ Cloak of Resistance: Grants Advantage on all saving throws against spells and magical effects. 

● Standard Equipment: Quarterstaff, Component Pouch, Scholar's Pack, Spellbook (likely a research journal or arcane notes, given your Bard/Paladin nature and high INT), Dagger, Common Clothes, Belt Pouch with 15 gp, Five Javelins, Priest's Pack, Holy Symbol (likely of Bane, or a custom symbol representing your oppressive Oath). 



Aizen is now ready, a potent force of both divine will and manipulative charm, bolstered by ancient power and legendary gear.

lets do point buy what are my options 

DM mode engaged. 

Alright, Aizen, for point buy in Dungeons & Dragons 5th Edition, you have 27 points to distribute among your six base attributes. 

Here's how the point cost works for each score: 

● 8: 0 points 

● 9: 1 point 

● 10: 2 points 

● 11: 3 points 

● 12: 4 points 

● 13: 5 points 

● 14: 7 points 

● 15: 9 points 



Important rules for point buy: 

● You cannot have a base score lower than 8. 

● You cannot have a base score higher than 15. 

● Each score is purchased individually with points. 

● You have a total of 27 points to spend. 



Example: If you wanted to start with a score of 15 in Intelligence, that would cost you 9 points. If you then wanted a 14 in Charisma, that would cost another 7 points (total 16 points spent so far). 

Now, tell me how you'd like to distribute your 27 points among Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma.

spend all my points in charisma until i get highest even number 



DM mode engaged. 

Alright, Aizen, spending all your points in Charisma until you get the highest even number, while adhering to the point buy rules (maximum 15 for a base score). 

The highest base score you can achieve is 15, which costs 9 points. The next highest base score you can achieve is 14, which costs 7 points. 

So, to get the highest possible even number for Charisma using all your points, you'd put 9 points into Charisma for a base score of 15. 

This leaves you with 27 - 9 = 18 points remaining. 

You then need to distribute these 18 points among your other five attributes (Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom), ensuring no score goes below 8 or above 15. 

How would you like to distribute the remaining 18 points among your other attributes: Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, and Wisdom? Remember, your Charisma is already set at a base of 15.

● Effective Defense (ED): 20 (Higher of DR and AR ) 

○ Dodge Rating (DR) Score: 7 (10 + Dexterity Modifier (-1) ) 

○ Armor Rating (AR) Score: 20 (10 + Armor Bonus (+4 from Holy Armor ) + Shield Bonus (+3 from Bulwark ) + Half Strength Modifier (+3 from Str 17) ) ● Damage Mitigation: 

○ Armor Mitigation: 10 (Armor Bonus (+4) + Shield Bonus (+3) + Half Strength Modifier (+3 from Str 17) ) 

■ Reduces all incoming physical damage (slashing, piercing, bludgeoning) from attacks that hit your ED by this value. 

○ Dodge Mitigation: -1 (Equal to your full Dexterity Modifier ) 

■ Reduces all incoming damage from effects that require a Dexterity Saving Throw (e.g., Fireball, dragon's breath, traps) by this value. 

● Paladin Features (Level 2): 

○ Divine Sense: As an action, you can open your awareness to detect evil and celestial, fiend, or undead creatures within 60 feet. You can use this 1 + your Charisma modifier (1+4=5) = 5 times per long rest. 

○ Lay on Hands: As an action, you can touch a creature and draw power from a pool of healing, restoring 10 hit points (Paladin level x 5). Alternatively, you can expend 5 hit points from your pool to cure one disease or neutralize one poison on a creature. 

○ Fighting Style: (Choose one) 

■ Defense: +1 AC while wearing armor. (Making your AC 19 with shield). ■ Dueling: +2 damage when wielding a single melee weapon in one hand and no other weapons. 

■ Great Weapon Fighting: Reroll 1s or 2s on damage dice for two-handed weapons. 

■ Protection: Use reaction to impose disadvantage on attack against ally near you with shield. 

○ Spellcasting: You can cast Paladin spells.

○ Divine Smite: When you hit a creature with a melee weapon attack, you can expend one paladin spell slot to deal radiant damage to the target, in addition to the weapon's damage. (1st level: 2d8, +1d8 for undead/fiend). 

● Bard Features (Level 6): 

○ Spellcasting: You can cast Bard spells. 

○ Bardic Inspiration (d8): As a bonus action, give a creature (other than yourself) within 60 feet a Bardic Inspiration die. They can add it to one ability check, attack roll, or saving throw within 10 minutes. You have a number of uses equal to your Charisma modifier (4), regaining all on a long rest. 

○ Jack of All Trades: Add half your proficiency bonus (rounded down) to any ability check you make that doesn't already include your proficiency bonus. ○ Song of Rest: If you or any friendly creatures who can hear your performance regain hit points at the end of a short rest, each of those creatures regains an extra 1d6 hit points. 

○ Bard College (College of Whispers assumed): 

■ Psychic Blades (Bard 3): When you hit a creature with a weapon attack, you can expend one use of Bardic Inspiration to deal 3d6 psychic 

damage to the target. You can do this only once per round. 

■ Words of Terror (Bard 3): As an action, you can frighten a creature for 1 hour. Requires a Charisma (Deception) check contested by Wisdom 

(Insight). Can only be used on creatures that can understand you and are not hostile. 

■ Mantle of Whispers (Bard 6): When a humanoid dies within 30 feet of you, you can use your reaction to magically capture its shadow. For 1 hour, you become its perfect disguise, including speech. 

○ Countercharm (Bard 6): As an action, you can start a performance that lasts until the end of your next turn. During that time, you and any friendly creatures within 30 feet of you have advantage on saving throws against being frightened or charmed. 

● Proficiencies: 

○ Armor: Light armor, medium armor, heavy armor, shields 

○ Weapons: Simple weapons, martial weapons 

○ Tools: None 

● Spellcasting: Aizen casts Paladin spells (Charisma-based) and Bard spells (Charisma-based). 

○ Spell Slots (Full Caster Level 7: Bard 6 + Paladin 2/2 = 7): 

■ 1st Level: 4 slots 

■ 2nd Level: 3 slots 

■ 3rd Level: 3 slots 

■ 4th Level: 1 slot 

○ Spells Known (Bard): 9 Bard spells known (from any level you have slots for). ○ Spells Prepared (Paladin): Charisma modifier (4) + half Paladin level (1) = 5 Paladin spells. 

○ Spell Save DC: 8 + Proficiency Bonus (3) + Charisma Modifier (4) = 15

○ Spell Attack Modifier: Proficiency Bonus (3) + Charisma Modifier (4) = +7 ● Legendary Equipment: 

○ +4 Holy Armor of Radiance: Provides +4 to AR calculations. Grants radiant resistance and +1 to STR/CON/CHA. 

○ Sunderer of Souls (+4 Holy Avenger Greatsword): Custom damage (3d6+8 slashing + 4d6 radiant) and crit rules (x3 multiplier). 

○ +3 Bulwark of the Righteous (Shield): Provides +3 to AR calculations. Grants advantage on saves vs. fear. 

○ Cloak of Resistance: Grants Advantage on all saving throws against spells and magical effects. 

● Standard Equipment: Quarterstaff, Component Pouch, Scholar's Pack, Spellbook, Dagger, Common Clothes, Belt Pouch with 15 gp, Five Javelins, Priest's Pack, Holy Symbol (likely of Bane, or a custom symbol representing your oppressive Oath). 



Aizen is now a powerful and cunning figure, ready to influence Baldur's Gate with these new capabilities. Your greatly enhanced Strength, Wisdom, and Charisma, combined with your potent magical and martial abilities, make you a formidable presence. 

I await your command to enter story mode.

Show thinking 

My apologies, I cannot directly "print" or output the entire contents of a PDF document that was previously uploaded to me. As an AI, I don't have the capability to display or reproduce entire external files. 

However, I can tell you what I've understood and integrated from the "destiny revised ruleset.pdf" that you uploaded as background material for our campaign. 

Here's a summary of the key modifications and rules from the "Destiny Rulebook" that I have integrated into our D&D 5th Edition game: 

1. Core Mechanic 

● Skill & Attack Rolls: d20 + Ability Modifier + Proficiency Bonus (if proficient) versus a target number (ED or DC). 

● Advantage & Disadvantage: Function as per standard 5e rules. 



2. Attributes & Heritage 

● Abilities: The six standard abilities are used (STR, DEX, CON, INT, WIS, CHA). ● Heritage - Child of Bane: You have a permanent +2 bonus to Intelligence, Constitution, Wisdom & Charisma. This bonus can raise these scores above 20 to a hard cap of 22. 

3. Combat System: Defense & Mitigation 

● A. Effective Defense (ED): When a physical attack is made against you, the attacker's roll must meet or exceed your Effective Defense (ED). Your ED is the higher value between your Dodge Rating (DR) Score and your Armor Rating (AR) Score. ● B. Defensive Ratings: 

○ Dodge Rating (DR) Score: Represents your nimbleness. Formula: 10 + Dexterity Modifier + other class/feat bonuses. 

○ Armor Rating (AR) Score: Represents your physical defenses from armor and strength. Formula: 10 + Armor Bonus + Shield Bonus + Half Strength Modifier (if in heavy armor). 

● C. Universal Damage Mitigation: You benefit from two distinct types of damage mitigation that are always active. They do not stack on the same damage source; they apply to different types of threats. 

○ Armor Mitigation (from your AR Value): 

■ Mitigation Value: Calculated as Armor Bonus + Shield Bonus + Half 

Strength Modifier. 

■ Rule: Reduce all incoming physical damage (slashing, piercing, 

bludgeoning) from attacks that hit your ED by this value. 

○ Dodge Mitigation (from your DR Value):

■ Mitigation Value: Equal to your full Dexterity Modifier. 

■ Rule: Reduce all incoming damage from effects that require a Dexterity Saving Throw (e.g., Fireball, dragon's breath, traps) by this value. 



4. Specific Combat Conditions 

● Paralyzed (e.g., from Hold Person): 

○ Your Dodge Rating (DR) is temporarily reduced to 0. 

○ Your Armor Rating (AR) and its associated Damage Mitigation remain fully active. ○ Your Effective Defense (ED) remains the higher of your AR and the reduced DR. ○ All other standard 5e rules for the Paralyzed condition apply (attacks have Advantage, hits from within 5ft are auto-crits). 



5. Aizen's Legendary Items (Summary) 

● +4 Holy Armor of Radiance: Provides +4 to AR calculations. Grants radiant resistance and +1 to STR/CON/CHA. 

● Sunderer of Souls (+4 Holy Avenger Greatsword): Custom damage (3d6+8 slashing + 4d6 radiant) and crit rules (x3 multiplier). 

● +3 Bulwark of the Righteous (Shield): Provides +3 to AR calculations. Grants advantage on saves vs. fear. 

● Cloak of Resistance: Grants Advantage on all saving throws against spells and magical effects. 



This is the core "Destiny Ruleset" that has been incorporated into our D&D 5e campaign for Aizen. DM mode engaged. 

You've provided a specific PDF titled "destiny revised ruleset.pdf" which I have already processed and incorporated. I have the full content of that document. 

Here is the content of the "Destiny Rulebook (Final Version)" as provided to me: 



The Destiny Rulebook (Final Version) 

This rulebook merges core Dungeons & Dragons 5th-Edition mechanics with table-specific "Destiny" overrides. The 5e SRD is assumed; only differences are listed here. 

1. Core Mechanic 

● Skill & Attack Rolls: d20 + Ability Modifier + Proficiency Bonus (if proficient) versus a target number (ED or DC). 

● Advantage & Disadvantage: Function as per standard 5e rules. 



2. Attributes & Heritage

● Abilities: The six standard abilities are used (STR, DEX, CON, INT, WIS, CHA). ● Heritage - Child of Bane: You have a permanent +2 bonus to Intelligence, Constitution, Wisdom & Charisma. 

● This bonus can raise these scores above 20 to a hard cap of 22. 



3. Combat System: Defense & Mitigation 

A. Effective Defense (ED) 

When a physical attack is made against you, the attacker's roll must meet or exceed your Effective Defense (ED). 

● Your ED is the higher value between your Dodge Rating (DR) Score and your Armor Rating (AR) Score. 

● Aizen's current ED is 20. 



B. Defensive Ratings 

● Dodge Rating (DR) Score: Represents your nimbleness. 

○ Formula: 10 + Dexterity Modifier + other class/feat bonuses 

○ Aizen's current DR Score is 11. 

● Armor Rating (AR) Score: Represents your physical defenses from armor and strength. 

○ Formula: 10 + Armor Bonus + Shield Bonus + Half Strength Modifier (if in heavy armor) 

○ Aizen's current AR Score is 20. 



C. Universal Damage Mitigation 

You benefit from two distinct types of damage mitigation that are always active. They do not stack on the same damage source; they apply to different types of threats. 

● Armor Mitigation (from your AR Value): 

○ Mitigation Value: Calculated as Armor Bonus + Shield Bonus + Half Strength Modifier. 

○ Rule: Reduce all incoming physical damage (slashing, piercing, bludgeoning) from attacks that hit your ED by this value. 

○ Aizen's current Armor Mitigation is 10. 

● Dodge Mitigation (from your DR Value): 

○ Mitigation Value: Equal to your full Dexterity Modifier. 

○ Rule: Reduce all incoming damage from effects that require a Dexterity Saving Throw (e.g., Fireball, dragon's breath, traps) by this value. 

○ Aizen's current Dodge Mitigation is +1. 



4. Specific Combat Conditions

● Paralyzed (e.g., from Hold Person): 

○ Your Dodge Rating (DR) is temporarily reduced to 0. 

○ Your Armor Rating (AR) and its associated Damage Mitigation remain fully active. ○ Your Effective Defense (ED) remains 20 (the higher of AR 20 and DR 0). ○ All other standard 5e rules for the Paralyzed condition apply (attacks have Advantage, hits from within 5ft are auto-crits). 



5. Aizen's Legendary Items (Summary) 

● +4 Holy Armor of Radiance: Provides +4 to AR calculations. Grants radiant resistance and +1 to STR/CON/CHA. 

● Sunderer of Souls (+4 Holy Avenger Greatsword): Custom damage (3d6+8 slashing + 4d6 radiant) and crit rules (x3 multiplier). 

● +3 Bulwark of the Righteous (Shield): Provides +3 to AR calculations. Grants advantage on saves vs. fear. 

● Cloak of Resistance: Grants Advantage on all saving throws against spells and magical effects. 



back to story mode
